sentences:
[G]: First proper Tiltak-Taktician match in a while, with much longer time control than previous matches. Tiltak has improved as expected, but Tako still gets plenty of wins

**Tiltak vs Taktician, 2000 games.**
**Size:** 6.
**Engine settings:** `-multi-cut` for Taktician, default settings for Tiltak
**Time control: **90+0.9.
**CPU: ** R9-5900X, 12(24) cores at 3.7GHz. 24 games were played in parallel.
**Opening book:** 6s_4ply_balanced_openings.txt. This book consists of 1000 openings, intended to have a smaller white advantage than the standard opening position. Each opening was played twice, with the engines switching sides.
**Results:** +1616-281=103, +279 elo for Tiltak. 953 white wins, 944 black wins.
[G]: I considered changing it, this time control may have been large enough for Tako to benefit from bigger TT
[G]: I tend to think it doesn't matter though, its TT is pretty large by default
[G]: The 20+0.2 match I ran a few days ago actually had slightly better result for Tako. Even though the 90+0.9 match had a slightly stronger Tiltak version (I think)
[D]: I think I might rather see 10x the time control, 1/10 the games. I don't really think there are so many unique openings in Tak unless you start getting into a ton of plies
[G]: I've wanted to try this in the past, the biggest reason I haven't is due to memory limitations. Tiltak and Tako are both pretty memory-hungry, and if you run 8 games in parallel, that's 16 engine instances that all want lots of RAM to themselves
[G]: I have a new shiny desktop with 64GB RAM now though. I can probably squeeze in a fair number of games in parallel
[G]: I'm not sure people would find it *that* interesting though, certainly not with the opening book I'm using now. They all start from really bad positions
[D]: Question: is it actually useful to run games on the "hyperthreading" cores?
[E]: i think seeing engines tackle weird positions is relatively interesting
[G]: You know, that's a very good question. I never tested it, I just did it anyway'
[G]: There's lots of candy for you in that 2000-game PTN then 😁
[E]: i don't know if i want to look through them one by one til i find something interesting tho
[E]: i suppose the tiltak losses ought to be a good place to start
[G]: They're certainly interesting for me. I use them a lot for development ideas
[E]: is that file here anywhere?
[G]: https://discord.com/channels/176389490762448897/932765511358513204/934815373340061727 I put it in a stupid place
[E]: sick
[D]: Yeah just curious, I don't exactly understand what "hyperthreading" does. My understanding is it makes it easier to context switch, but doesn't directly improve the computing power of the machine. And considering that we should be running at full blast on the cores we're using, my guess was it wouldn't help. But that's based on my understanding of hyperthreading, which is at least oversimplified if not wrong.
[E]: i think it's wrong
[G]: I never understood hyperthreading properly either. But I always thought it was free multithreading performance, at least if each thread can use different execution ports on the CPU. One thread can do float math while the other is doing integer math, one thread can do whatever when the other is waiting on a cache miss, etc
[E]: it's about one core executing two threads in parallel
[E]: sharing the internal resources of the core (decoder, ALUs, etc.)

sentences:
[G]: First proper Tiltak-Taktician match in a while, with much longer time control than previous matches. Tiltak has improved as expected, but Tako still gets plenty of wins

**Tiltak vs Taktician, 2000 games.**
**Size:** 6.
**Engine settings:** `-multi-cut` for Taktician, default settings for Tiltak
**Time control: **90+0.9.
**CPU: ** R9-5900X, 12(24) cores at 3.7GHz. 24 games were played in parallel.
**Opening book:** 6s_4ply_balanced_openings.txt. This book consists of 1000 openings, intended to have a smaller white advantage than the standard opening position. Each opening was played twice, with the engines switching sides.
**Results:** +1616-281=103, +279 elo for Tiltak. 953 white wins, 944 black wins.
[G]: I considered changing it, this time control may have been large enough for Tako to benefit from bigger TT
[G]: I tend to think it doesn't matter though, its TT is pretty large by default
[G]: The 20+0.2 match I ran a few days ago actually had slightly better result for Tako. Even though the 90+0.9 match had a slightly stronger Tiltak version (I think)
[D]: I think I might rather see 10x the time control, 1/10 the games. I don't really think there are so many unique openings in Tak unless you start getting into a ton of plies
[G]: I've wanted to try this in the past, the biggest reason I haven't is due to memory limitations. Tiltak and Tako are both pretty memory-hungry, and if you run 8 games in parallel, that's 16 engine instances that all want lots of RAM to themselves
[G]: I have a new shiny desktop with 64GB RAM now though. I can probably squeeze in a fair number of games in parallel
[G]: I'm not sure people would find it *that* interesting though, certainly not with the opening book I'm using now. They all start from really bad positions
[D]: Question: is it actually useful to run games on the "hyperthreading" cores?
[E]: i think seeing engines tackle weird positions is relatively interesting
[G]: You know, that's a very good question. I never tested it, I just did it anyway'
[G]: There's lots of candy for you in that 2000-game PTN then 😁
[E]: i don't know if i want to look through them one by one til i find something interesting tho
[E]: i suppose the tiltak losses ought to be a good place to start
[G]: They're certainly interesting for me. I use them a lot for development ideas
[E]: is that file here anywhere?
[G]: https://discord.com/channels/176389490762448897/932765511358513204/934815373340061727 I put it in a stupid place
[E]: sick
[D]: Yeah just curious, I don't exactly understand what "hyperthreading" does. My understanding is it makes it easier to context switch, but doesn't directly improve the computing power of the machine. And considering that we should be running at full blast on the cores we're using, my guess was it wouldn't help. But that's based on my understanding of hyperthreading, which is at least oversimplified if not wrong.
[E]: i think it's wrong
[G]: I never understood hyperthreading properly either. But I always thought it was free multithreading performance, at least if each thread can use different execution ports on the CPU. One thread can do float math while the other is doing integer math, one thread can do whatever when the other is waiting on a cache miss, etc
[E]: it's about one core executing two threads in parallel
[E]: sharing the internal resources of the core (decoder, ALUs, etc.)
[G]: And modern CPUs have like 10 execution ports, so they can share more granular stuff than "float math" or "int math". Or so I think
[F]: well every core has limited resources, and hyperthreading also allows for multiple threads to share those resources reducing idle time. imagine there being 3 float multiply units, and if one thread doesn't use all of them the other might. Thats really oversimplified but one thing that works towards performance increase
[D]: Well because of pipelining I imagine it's less dramatic an improvement than it sounds like.
[E]: if both threads are executing a high latency workload (e.g. memory accesses), then it's likely to be significant
[E]: bordering on 2x, in the optimal case
[G]: Since this appears to have piqued everyone's curiosity, I will test Tiltak's hyperthreading scaling
[D]: Yeah I guess you raise a good point about memory access.
[G]: I'm also not sure the pipeline mental model is accurate anymore. To my understanding, modern CPUs can execute instructions truly in parallel, e.g. issue several instructions on the same clock cycle
[D]: Probably some, not others. Just to make it more inscrutable.
[F]: they usually fetch multiple instructions each cycle. the biggest difference to the traditional pipeline is the massive amount of reordering of instructions that happens in a processor. basically for every instruction, the dependencies are "calculated" and then they are thrown onto a big pile. next step in the pipeline picks as many instructions as the resources can handle in the next cycle which dependencies are resolved (or going to be resolved soon enough)
[G]: I created the following ad-hoc benchmark: Run Tiltak for 1m nodes from the 6s starting position, and print the result. Do this 24 times, with either 12 or 24 threads. I ran the benchmark 3 times for each case.

12 threads:
```
- 17.10s
- 17.16s
- 17.21s
```
24 threads:
```
...
[G]: I haven't actually measured this either, but I don't think Tiltak spends much time at all on memory accesses. It does spend time on stupid stuff like floating-point division though, that seems like a good candidate for hyperthreading
[F]: 18% is still something
